title: Scope and data access review
content: Slack Marketplace apps should follow least privilege. Apps should not request broad workspace message or file access without a clear tested use case. User token scopes should only be used when the app needs to act from the user's perspective and bot token scopes are insufficient. Submitted scopes should map to functionality reviewers can test.

title: Public landing, support, and privacy pages
content: A Slack app should provide a public landing page explaining the app and install path, a public support page with a way to contact support without requiring an additional account, and a privacy policy explaining collected data, usage, retention, deletion, and contact methods.

title: AI component disclosures
content: Slack apps exposing users to generative AI should be transparent about actions, disclose model use, data retention, whether user data is used by the LLM, data tenancy and residency where relevant, and should not use Slack data to train large language models. AI should not make consequential decisions without human review.

title: Security and TLS
content: Slack app endpoints for commands, events, and interactivity should verify request authenticity and use TLS for traffic between Slack and the app. Production submissions should not depend on localhost or unsecured HTTP request URLs.

title: MCP tool discovery and metadata
content: MCP tools should be discoverable with clear names, titles, descriptions, input schemas, and accurate annotations. Read-only hints should match actual behavior. Tools that mutate state, create records, delete records, send messages, or trigger external effects should not be marked read-only and should make write behavior clear to users.

title: Slack Agent and Assistant experience
content: A Slack Agent should provide a useful native interaction surface, clear onboarding, helpful responses, and visible progress while work is running. The experience should function consistently inside Slack and should not leave users at dead ends.

title: Real-Time Search and zero-copy context
content: Real-Time Search is intended to retrieve relevant Slack context in response to a user interaction without storing customer message content on an external server. Apps should request only the granular search scopes needed and return source links or context that the user can access.

title: Real-Time Search request authorization
content: Real-Time Search calls made with a bot token require an action token from an eligible message or app mention event. User-token calls act on behalf of the authenticating user. Apps should preserve Slack access boundaries and avoid using broad history access as a substitute for the secure search interface.

title: Slack MCP identity and authorization
content: Slack MCP clients must use a registered Slack app identity and authorization that matches the user and workspace context. MCP tool access should be explicit, auditable, and limited to the scopes required by the tools that are actually demonstrated.

title: Slack privacy model
content: A Slack app should not expose private channel names, messages, files, or capabilities to people who could not access them in Slack. Shared-channel and multi-workspace behavior must preserve the access rights of the relevant user and workspace.

title: AI output and human review
content: Generative AI output may be inaccurate and should be clearly described. AI applications must not make consequential decisions without human review. SecureLore should present recommendations and evidence for an administrator rather than silently approving or denying a deployment.
